questions.prompt: Selenium's findElements(...) returns a collection of matched elements. Which type is it?
options: A Map of elements keyed by id
A List (e.g. ArrayList) — ordered and accessed by index
A single WebElement
A boolean
questions.explanation: findElements returns a List<WebElement> — ordered and resizable — which you iterate over. findElement (singular) returns one element.

questions.prompt: You want to store test data you can look up by field name, like get("username"). Which collection fits?
options: A List
A Map (e.g. HashMap) of key-value pairs
An int array
A boolean
questions.explanation: A Map stores unique keys mapped to values, so you fetch by key with get(...). A List is indexed by position, not by name.

questions.prompt: In try/catch/finally, what is guaranteed about the finally block?
options: It runs only if no exception was thrown
It runs only if an exception was thrown
It always runs, whether or not an exception occurred — ideal for cleanup
It runs before the try block
questions.explanation: finally executes regardless of outcome, which is why cleanup like driver.quit() belongs there.

questions.prompt: Selenium cannot find an element you reference. What happens, and why does it matter to a tester?
options: Nothing — the line is skipped silently
It throws a NoSuchElementException; catching it lets the test fail cleanly and clean up instead of crashing
It returns null and the test always passes
The browser closes automatically and the test passes
questions.explanation: A missing locator throws NoSuchElementException. Handling it turns an abrupt crash into a clear, reported failure with proper cleanup.

questions.prompt: Why put driver.quit() in finally rather than on the line right after the try block?
options: finally code runs faster
If an exception is thrown in the try, code after it is skipped — but finally still runs, so the browser still closes
driver.quit() is not allowed outside finally
It makes no difference at all
questions.explanation: An exception jumps out of the try, skipping any code after it. finally always runs, guaranteeing the browser is closed even on failure.
